reasoning:
The question asks whether a qualifying military encounter between China and the Philippines will occur between January 29 and December 31, 2025. Per the resolution criteria, the encounter must involve actual military forces (PCG does not count, though CCG does) and must involve use of force such as missile strikes, gunfire, or intentional ramming causing significant damage/sinking.  Extensive search of events in 2025 confirms that while there have been numerous escalations in the South China Sea (including water cannons, aerial patrols, and ship collisions in August and October 2025), none have met the criteria. The October 2025 ramming incident involved a Philippine civilian/government vessel (BFAR), not a military force, and resulted in only minor damage. Another collision in August primarily involved two Chinese ships hitting each other.

The Polymarket price for this question on November 13, 2025, was 0.03, indicating that traders recognized no qualifying trigger had occurred and assessed a ~3% risk for the remainder of the year. Ten more days have passed since then with no qualifying clashes. With only 38 days left in the year, the base rate of a sudden military escalation involving actual gunfire or catastrophic ramming remains very low, as both sides deliberately restrict actions to "gray-zone" non-military tactics to avoid triggering mutual defense treaties. Therefore, I estimate the probability of a qualifying event occurring before December 31, 2025, at ~3%.
